name: Yucatec Mexican Spanish

ancestors.notes: General Latin American Spanish (koine) — seseo/yeismo baseline shared by all LatAm varieties (Lipski 1994).
Yucatec Maya indigenous adstrate (regional contact influence; see stub yua.json).

notes: Yucatan-Peninsula Spanish — Lipski's (1994) 'Guatemala, parts of the Yucatan, and Costa Rica' zone; strong Yucatec Maya substrate. Over the pan-Latin-American baseline (seseo: c/z->/s/, yeismo: ll->/ʝ/) this spec models the four attested Yucatecan substrate reflexes: (1) SIGNATURE word/phrase-final /n/->[m] bilabialisation (positional_graphemes n.word_final; lope_blanch1987, michnowicz2008); (2) OCCLUSIVE intervocalic /b d g/ -> [b d g] instead of the Castilian spirants [β ð ɣ] (positional_graphemes b/d/g.intervocalic + d.word_final; michnowicz2012 p.105-106, lope_blanch1987); (3) aspirated /x/->[h] glottal jota, shared with the Mexican southern coasts (allophones x->h; lipski1994, lope_blanch1987). Glottalised/reinforced voiceless stops and glottal-stop hiatus insertion are attested but sociolinguistically stratified and variable (a minority realisation declining in apparent time, michnowicz2012 p.106, p.108-110), so they are documented here but NOT applied as a deterministic majority rule. All three modelled reflexes are themselves variable and stratified (younger/urban speakers converge on standard approximants and [x]; michnowicz2012 p.108-110); the spec encodes the traditional peninsular majority variant. Ancestry: es-ES-x-medieval -> es-ES -> es-419 -> es-MX -> es-MX-x-yucatan, with the Yucatec Maya substrate.

sources.author: Lipski, John M.
sources.title: Geographical and Social Varieties of Spanish: An Overview
sources.publisher: In J.I. Hualde, A. Olarrea & E. O'Rourke (eds.), The Handbook of Hispanic Linguistics, Wiley-Blackwell
sources.notes: READ (geo.pdf). Reproduces the Lipski (1994) geographical dialect-zone classification of Latin American Spanish (list (2)): Mexican/SW-US; Caribbean; Guatemala-Yucatan-Costa Rica; El Salvador-Honduras-Nicaragua; interior Colombia + highland Venezuela; Pacific coast Colombia/Ecuador/Peru; Andean (Ecuador/Peru/Bolivia/NW-Argentina/NE-Chile); Chile; Paraguay/NE-Argentina/E-Bolivia; Argentina/Uruguay. Used here to enumerate the regional zones; no phonology quoted.

sources.author: Lipski, John M.
sources.title: Latin American Spanish
sources.notes: The comprehensive dialect survey underlying the zone classification (cited via the author's own overview, geo.pdf, which was read). Documents the Yucatan peninsula sub-variety: retained coda /s/, seseo, and the glottal [h] realisation of /x/ shared with the Mexican southern coasts (as against highland velar [x]). Cited here for the /x/->[h] jota reflex and the conserved-/s/ peninsular baseline.

sources.author: Michnowicz, Jim
sources.title: The Standardization of Yucatan Spanish: Family Case Studies in Izamal and Merida
sources.publisher: In K. Geeslin & M. Diaz-Campos (eds.), Selected Proceedings of the 14th Hispanic Linguistics Symposium, Cascadilla Proceedings Project
sources.notes: READ (deposited papers/michnowicz2012_standardization_yucatan_spanish_HLS14.pdf). Three Maya-contact phonological features of Yucatan Spanish enumerated p.103-104: (a) occlusive /b d g/ in non-neutralising (e.g. intervocalic) contexts where standard Spanish has spirants [β ð ɣ] (p.105-106, Figure 4); (b) hiatus across word boundaries via glottal-stop insertion (p.106, Figure 5); (c) aspiration of voiceless /k/ (p.106, Figure 6). Apparent-time results (p.108-110): non-standard stop/glottal/aspiration variants decline in the youngest speakers (e.g. 21M 16%, 25M 11% occlusive average), i.e. the substrate reflexes are variable and stratified; the traditional peninsular majority variant is modelled here. Rising final -m noted p.113. Cited for occlusive /b d g/ and for the variable status of glottalisation.

sources.author: Michnowicz, Jim
sources.title: Final -m in Yucatan Spanish: A rapid and anonymous survey
sources.publisher: In J. Bruhn de Garavito & E. Valenzuela (eds.), Selected Proceedings of the 10th Hispanic Linguistics Symposium, Cascadilla Proceedings Project
sources.notes: Dedicated study of the signature Yucatecan reflex: absolute word/phrase-final /n/ realised as bilabial [m] (e.g. pan [pam]), a Yucatec-Maya contact feature increasing in apparent time as a marker of regional identity. Cited for the final /n/->[m] rule (positional_graphemes n.word_final).

sources.author: Lope Blanch, Juan M.
sources.title: Estudios sobre el espanol de Yucatan
sources.notes: Foundational descriptive survey of Yucatecan Spanish phonetics. Documents the polimorfismo yucateco: word-final nasal realisations dominated by [n] (~73%) with [m] (~12%) and [ŋ] (~8%); occlusive/reinforced /b d g/ with occlusion decreasing front-to-back (bilabial > dental > velar); glottalised and aspirated voiceless stops /p t k/ and glottal closure at vowel junctures; glottal [h] for /x/. Cited for final /n/->[m], occlusive /b d g/, /x/->[h] and the attested-but-variable glottalisation.

location.notes: Mérida (Yucatán) — the centre of the Yucatán peninsula. [Wikidata Q165204 P625]
